title: Here Are Plans to Dispel the Darkness and Make Hearts Blossom!
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The Tamil below is a source-checked reading copy: the opening salutation omitted by OCR has been restored, severely damaged phrases have been transcribed from the printed pages, and clear OCR word-break, character, and spacing errors have been repaired. The canonical source file remains unchanged. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement.
paragraphs: Each day I speak about public issues and the disorder of the Government functioning in Tamil Nadu. Today I want to share with you details of campaign meetings that our movement must plan and conduct.

Udanpirappē, you know that I have completed ninety years and am now in my ninety-first. Even at this age I cannot sleep well. I am not worried about my household. I worry about our party, and about the lull that has arisen because the campaign meetings which serve as its sword and shield have not for some time been held regularly and widely.

Was it not the ideological campaign carried from town to town and village to village by our speakers that raised and developed the DMK to this height? Whether or not I can travel and campaign at my age, I continue to issue the movement's views through statements and letters to Udanpirappugal. Apart from Murasoli, other newspapers, fearing the rulers, do not publish them fully. Merely reading my statements continuously will provide ample material for campaigning.

Periyar described the Dravidar Kazhagam itself as a campaign organisation: one dedicated to removing long-standing ignorance, barriers to progress and social defects; uniting all people into a society; and eradicating the social degradation, religious superstition and caste division afflicting Dravidian people, freeing them from upper-caste oppression.

When the Dravida Munnetra Kazhagam was formed from the Dravidar Kazhagam, Arignar Anna called it a grafted plant and described the DK and DMK as a double-barrelled gun. The DMK has therefore always been regarded as an extension of the Dravidar Kazhagam. Anna called it a training camp and believed disciplined training essential to propaganda.

Campaigning is indispensable to winning ideals, and it requires many people trained in that camp. Anna explained that an organisation brings together many minds and combines many people's strength in a common camp. A camp needs different weapons and capable fighters. What use is a camp stacked with swords but no shields, bows but no arrows, guns but no ammunition, or all of them in order but no fighters able to use them? He thus explained the need for varied material, intelligent and eloquent speakers able to handle it, and continuous, coordinated campaigning across the country.

DMK campaigners became known among the public as “campaign cannons” and “street-corner balladeers.” DMK meetings were regarded as the movement's evening colleges. Before the DMK entered politics, a platform address was called a sermon and its speaker a preacher. It was our movement's speakers who transformed the sermon into an oration and the preacher into an orator in good Tamil.

The astonishing campaign methods employed by Anna and his gifted brothers overturned Tamil Nadu politics. Government, democracy, voting rights and freedom of expression ceased to seem like foreign objects imported from elsewhere. People understood that these belonged to them and served the common good. Their awakening and resurgence came from the DMK's tireless campaign.
The movement flourished like a banyan because propaganda reached every settlement, street corner and veranda. Every election victory up to 1996 came through that strength.

Until his last breath, Periyar travelled ceaselessly in his van campaigning. When he died, Tamil Nadu grieved that “Periyar's campaign tour has stopped.” Anna tirelessly travelled across Tamil Nadu and campaigned through the night. He proudly recalled, “We campaigned all night, reached Thozhudur, and dawn broke.”

I too travelled without sleep through forests, fields, villages, hamlets and cities. I rode bicycles and bullock carts over roads filled with stones and thorns and addressed meetings under Petromax lamps. Times have changed. Transport, stages, publicity and meeting arrangements have changed. I continually used drama, cinema, writing and the platform as instruments of propaganda. I also met with many accidents and suffered many losses on those tours.

After 1996, not only print newspapers but electronic media began playing a major role in elections. Some newspapers had opposed the DMK from the beginning and displayed an allergy and hostility towards it. Most people uplifted by the DMK's awakening were Backward Classes, Most Backward Classes and Scheduled Castes. Certain social sections could not accept the DMK's social ideas and political principles, and treated each step of its progress as thunder falling on their heads.

Newspapers controlled by that small group repeatedly tried to destroy the movement, almost as though bound by an unwritten agreement. New media adopted the same approach. The DMK possessed neither newspaper power nor media strength.

Some newspapers and media now challenge democratic values. They use freedom of expression to impose their own views and occupy people's thinking. Abandoning the basic purpose of reporting facts, avoiding likes and dislikes, offering dignified criticism and enriching public thought, they distort information, bend it to their wishes and mix it with invention.

Commercial rivalry intensifies this conduct: who reports first, who attracts more listeners, whose story pleases more people? Making a speck into a mountain, a splinter into a pillar, a cat into a tiger, or raising dust and depicting it as a monster has become routine. By withholding the full facts, they create confusion until people cannot distinguish butter from lime.

The DMK Government of 1996-2001 advanced Tamil Nadu on many fronts, surpassed every development measure, and made it a leading State. Yet some media deliberately spread the fiction that no money circulated under DMK rule, persuaded people, and caused its electoral defeat.

Did the 2006-2011 DMK Government lack achievements? The 2G allegation was like a mirage deer searching for mirage water. Yet they let that speculative monster roam, frightened and diverted people, and defeated the DMK. Exaggeration, wild imagination and their seductive presentation tied voters' eyes and hid our achievements. Thinking deeply about the victory of that few and the electoral defeat of our many reveals the background.

The media now support a person convicted in a corruption case and create the illusion of a sympathy wave. They elevate those who claim the conviction was a conspiracy and condemn the judge. At the same time, they inflate another case whose trial is unfinished and hurry to pronounce judgment themselves.

They seek to make law, fairness and justice mean one thing for them and another for us. They try to persuade people that equality before law is itself mistaken. By repeatedly claiming that no public anger exists against a Government filled with failures and corruption, they confuse even clear-minded people into wondering whether that claim might be true.

Our only weapon for changing this situation is campaigning. Every DMK member must become a campaigner. We must abandon the practice of holding meetings only when headquarters directs us. Meetings must be conducted every day across the State.

Within the coming year the DMK should hold at least five thousand public meetings. The movement now has sixty-five district organisations, including newly divided ones, 578 union organisations, 129 town organisations, 122 area organisations and 538 town-panchayat organisations. If the 578 unions, 129 towns and 122 areas each hold one meeting a month, that produces 829 meetings monthly. Over twelve months, that is 829 × 12 = 9,948 meetings, nearly ten thousand campaign meetings.

To strengthen and carry forward the campaign, I lovingly ask every union, town and area organisation to hold one meeting each month from January 2015 and create new momentum. After each meeting, send its details to headquarters. The general secretary, Professor, the treasurer and I will examine the monthly computerised summaries.

Let me recall what I said at a wedding on 20 November 2014. A DMK meeting must not be approached merely as one intended to change a Government. It must be regarded as a campaign that creates the conditions for social revolution, transforms society at its roots, and creates a society of dignity. Our meetings must advance Self-Respect ideas, social ideas and the principles necessary for social transformation.
The previous day, Professor, Tamil leader Veeramani and I sat at my home and considered what more should be done to reform society. I am issuing these views on the basis of that discussion.

Only by classifying and repeating our ideas at the continuous meetings to be held across the State under this programme will they take firm root in people's minds and open the doors of change. Let the movement's ideas shine like a lamp upon a hill. Let darkness recede. Let hearts blossom!
With affection,
M.K.
